items.title: CVE-2026-31431: CISA Adds Actively Exploited Linux Root Access Bug to KEV
items.summary: CISA added CVE-2026-31431, a local privilege escalation flaw in the Linux kernel (CVSS 7.8), to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. The bug allows an unprivileged local user to gain root access; patches land in Linux kernel versions 6.18.22, 6.19.12, and 7.0.
items.why: Active exploitation of a Linux kernel root bug puts millions of servers, cloud VMs, and containers at immediate risk—any low-privileged attacker or malware already on a system can escalate to full root control.

items.title: Microsoft Warns of Two Actively Exploited Defender Zero-Days (CVE-2026-41091 & CVE-2026-45498) — CISA Deadline June 3
items.summary: Microsoft disclosed two actively exploited privilege escalation flaws in Microsoft Defender—CVE-2026-41091 and CVE-2026-45498—both added to CISA's KEV catalog with a federal remediation deadline of June 3, 2026. A third Defender zero-day remains unpatched and in the wild.
items.why: Defender is installed on hundreds of millions of Windows systems; SYSTEM-level escalation lets adversaries disable endpoint protections, persist stealthily, and pivot laterally before defenders can respond.

items.title: APT28 Weaponizes CVE-2026-21509 Within 24 Hours, Hits 9 Eastern European Nations via Cloud C2
items.summary: Russia's APT28 (Fancy Bear) exploited a newly disclosed Microsoft Office vulnerability (CVE-2026-21509) within 24 hours of its public revelation, running a concentrated 72-hour spear-phishing campaign (Jan 28–30, 2026) against military and government entities across nine Eastern European nations. The group routed C2 traffic through legitimate cloud services to evade detection.
items.why: APT28's sub-24-hour exploitation timeline demonstrates nation-state actors can convert disclosures into active weapons faster than most patch cycles allow—and cloud-hosted C2 infrastructure makes blocking or attributing the campaign significantly harder.

items.title: CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report: eCrime Breakout Time Drops 65% to Just 29 Minutes
items.summary: CrowdStrike's 2026 Global Threat Report reveals the average eCrime breakout time—initial access to lateral movement—fell to 29 minutes in 2025, a 65% speed increase over 2024. Adversaries are integrating AI tools for evasion, exploiting trusted relationships, and targeting security blind spots; 28.3% of CVEs were exploited within 24 hours of disclosure.
items.why: A 29-minute lateral movement window leaves almost no time for human-led incident response—organizations without automated detection and containment will be unable to stop spread before significant damage is done.

items.title: Mandiant M-Trends 2026: Dwell Time Rises to 14 Days as Attackers Systematically Destroy Backup Infrastructure
items.summary: Mandiant's M-Trends 2026, based on 500,000+ hours of incident response across 2025, shows global median dwell time climbed to 14 days. A defining trend is threat actors deliberately targeting backup systems and identity services to deny recovery, with credential stealers rising to 9% of observed malware families (up from 5% in 2024).
items.why: Systematically destroying backups breaks the traditional incident response playbook—organizations must adopt immutable, air-gapped backups and assume identity infrastructure will be compromised during any serious breach.
